file_level.general_notes: Best practices score: 8.5/10 (0 FAIL, 4 WARN, 13 PASS)

rules.detail: No evidence of implicit-any or other strict-mode violations within this file. tsconfig is not provided here but the code itself is consistent with strict mode enabled.
All variadic parameters correctly typed as `unknown[]` rather than `any[]`. No explicit or implicit `any` found.
No type assertions or union types present. Not applicable to this utility module.
No obvious opportunity for Pick/Omit/Partial/Record here. The data shape is intentionally minimal.

The `listeners` field is never reassigned after construction; it should be declared `readonly`. The Map reference itself is stable — only its contents change, so `readonly` is both accurate and self-documenting.
`EventHandler` is defined as a `type` alias, which is idiomatic for function signatures. Consistent with a type-first convention; no interfaces mixed in.
No obvious lint violations: consistent semicolons, arrow functions, no unused variables, no dangling commas in bad positions. Code is clean.

Neither `SpinEventEmitter` (the class) nor its public methods (`on`, `off`, `emit`) have JSDoc comments. `SPIN_DONE` also lacks a brief description. Consumers must infer semantics from the ADR rather than inline docs.
Uses `Map`, nullish coalescing (`??`), rest/spread, and `for...of` — all idiomatic modern TypeScript. No deprecated APIs.
No imports in this file. File placement (`src/events.ts`) is consistent with the documented project structure.

The `emit` loop invokes handlers bare, with no try-catch isolation. If any handler throws synchronously, subsequent registered handlers are silently skipped. Per ADR-004, SPIN_DONE consumers include logging, analytics, and free-spin chaining — all of which are safety-critical in a regulated gaming context. A single bad handler can silently break the entire observation chain.
No hardcoded secrets, no `eval`, no command injection. This file does not contain RNG logic (that is in `rng.ts`), so regulated-gaming RNG rules do not apply here.
`off()` allocates a new filtered array on each removal — acceptable for a low-frequency lifecycle emitter. No N+1, no synchronous I/O, no unnecessary re-renders.
`SpinEventEmitter` is a plain class with no external dependencies — fully instantiable and testable in isolation. `on`/`off`/`emit` are straightforward to exercise.
No forced opportunities for `satisfies`, `const` type parameters, or `using` in this file. The simplicity is appropriate; adding newer syntax would be over-engineering for a 27-line utility.

The event map is structurally untyped: `on(event: string, handler: (...args: unknown[]) => void)`. Consumers of `SPIN_DONE` must unsafely cast `args[0]` to `SpinResult` without any compiler guarantee. A typed event-map pattern (`EventMap extends Record<string, unknown[]>`) would propagate the correct payload type to each listener at compile time, eliminating the runtime assumption.

suggestions.description: Mark `listeners` as `readonly` — the Map reference is never reassigned, only mutated in place.
suggestions.before: private listeners: Map<string, EventHandler[]> = new Map();
suggestions.after: private readonly listeners: Map<string, EventHandler[]> = new Map();

suggestions.description: Isolate handler errors inside `emit` so one throwing listener cannot silently drop all subsequent ones (critical for analytics/free-spin chaining per ADR-004).
Introduce a typed event-map so consumers receive the correct payload type without casting `unknown`.
Add JSDoc to public exports so API consumers do not need to consult the ADR.
